name: barbell one arm snatch
instructions: Stand with your feet shoulder-width apart, toes pointing slightly outwards.
Hold the barbell with an overhand grip, hands slightly wider than shoulder-width apart.
Bend your knees and lower your hips into a squat position, keeping your back straight and chest up.
Explosively extend your hips, knees, and ankles, driving the barbell upwards.
As the barbell reaches chest level, pull it upwards with your arm, keeping it close to your body.
Rotate your elbow under the barbell and extend your arm fully overhead, locking out your elbow.
Lower the barbell back down to the starting position in a controlled manner.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions, then switch arms.

Stand with your feet shoulder-width apart, toes pointing slightly outwards. Hold the barbell with an overhand grip, hands slightly wider than shoulder-width apart. Bend your knees and lower your hips into a squat position, keeping your back straight and chest up. Explosively extend your hips, knees, and ankles, driving the barbell upwards. As the barbell reaches chest level, pull it upwards with your arm, keeping it close to your body. Rotate your elbow under the barbell and extend your arm fully overhead, locking out your elbow. Lower the barbell back down to the starting position in a controlled manner. Repeat for the desired number of repetitions, then switch arms.

conditionNotes.note: Favor symmetrical loading and supported variations if fatigue changes your posture.
Use pain-free range of motion, slower tempo, and lighter progressions when joints feel irritated.
Weight-bearing resistance exercises are beneficial for bone density when load is appropriate.
Avoid loaded spinal flexion and excessive hinge depth. Brace core and use supported positions.
Maintain consistent breathing throughout. Avoid breath-holding.
Both aerobic and resistance training improve insulin sensitivity. Monitor blood sugar before, during, and after sessions. Have a fast-acting carb available.
Rhythm-based exercises and large-amplitude movements are beneficial. Focus on motor confidence.
Focus on muscle co-contraction and joint stability. Avoid locking out joints passively.
Functional patterns are generally safe. Prioritize pelvic floor awareness and avoid Valsalva.
Start light with higher reps and shorter ROM. Progress load gradually as pain-free capacity increases.
